note: Hand-annotated ground truth for Devlin et al. 2019 (BERT). Independent pass — overlaps with the generated extraction but deliberately phrases some claims differently and includes claims the generated file skipped, so eval produces non-trivial P/R.

claims.statement: Language model pre-training improves performance on a broad range of NLP tasks.
Pre-trained language representations are applied via two strategies: feature-based (ELMo-style) and fine-tuning (GPT-style).
Current fine-tuning approaches are constrained by unidirectional language-model pre-training, which is particularly harmful for token-level tasks like question answering.
We propose BERT, which uses a masked language model (MLM) objective inspired by the Cloze task to enable deep bidirectional pre-training.
BERT also uses a next-sentence-prediction task to jointly pre-train text-pair representations.

BERT is the first fine-tuning based representation model achieving state-of-the-art performance on a large suite of sentence- and token-level tasks, advancing the state of the art on eleven NLP tasks.
Pre-trained representations reduce the need for many heavily engineered task-specific architectures.
The BERT framework has two steps: unsupervised pre-training on unlabeled text followed by fine-tuning from the pre-trained weights on each downstream task's labeled data.
BERT's architecture is a multi-layer bidirectional Transformer encoder.
Two model sizes: BERT-BASE (L=12, H=768, A=12, 110M parameters) matched to GPT for comparison, and BERT-LARGE (L=24, H=1024, A=16, 340M parameters).
BERT uses bidirectional self-attention throughout, where GPT uses left-only constrained self-attention.
Input is encoded with WordPiece, sentence pairs are packed with [SEP] separators, and every sequence starts with a special [CLS] classification token.
For the masked LM, 15% of tokens are chosen at random for prediction; of those, 80% are replaced with [MASK], 10% with a random token, and 10% left unchanged.
For next-sentence prediction, sentence pairs are labeled IsNext half the time and NotNext (a random sentence) the other half.
The pre-training corpus is BooksCorpus (800M words) plus English Wikipedia (2,500M words), using only contiguous passages.
Fine-tuning is inexpensive: all paper results can be reproduced in at most one hour on a single Cloud TPU.
Using a document-level corpus (rather than shuffled sentences) is critical for extracting long contiguous sequences during pre-training.
BERT-BASE and BERT-LARGE beat all prior systems on every GLUE task, with 4.5% and 7.0% average accuracy improvement over prior state of the art respectively.
BERT obtains 4.6% absolute accuracy improvement on MNLI, the largest GLUE task.
BERT-LARGE significantly outperforms BERT-BASE across all tasks, with the largest gaps on tasks with little training data.
On SQuAD v1.1, BERT beats the top leaderboard entry by +1.5 F1 ensembled and +1.3 F1 as a single system.
On SQuAD v2.0, BERT shows a +5.1 F1 improvement over the previous best system.
On SWAG, BERT-LARGE outperforms the ESIM+ELMo baseline by 27.1% and OpenAI GPT by 8.3%.
Removing the next-sentence-prediction task significantly hurts QNLI, MNLI, and SQuAD 1.1 performance.
A left-to-right pre-trained model is worse than the MLM-pre-trained model on every task, with large drops on MRPC and SQuAD.
Adding a BiLSTM on top of the LTR model helps SQuAD but hurts GLUE; it does not recover the bidirectional MLM model's performance.
Scaling BERT to extreme model sizes yields large improvements on very small-scale downstream tasks, provided the model is sufficiently pre-trained.
We hypothesize that with full fine-tuning and few added parameters, even tiny labeled datasets benefit from larger pre-trained representations.
Using BERT as a frozen feature extractor (concat of top four hidden layers) comes within 0.3 F1 of full fine-tuning on CoNLL-2003 NER.
The main contribution is generalizing rich unsupervised-pre-training findings to deep bidirectional architectures, so a single pre-trained model can handle a broad range of NLP tasks.
